content: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy, incidence 1-2/100,000/year. Classic form is acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP); variants include acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN), Miller Fisher syndrome (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia). Pathophysiology: molecular mimicry—antecedent infection (Campylobacter jejuni most common, also CMV, EBV, Zika, Mycoplasma) triggers antibodies against gangliosides on peripheral nerves, causing complement-mediated damage. Clinical features: acute/subacute progressive symmetric weakness ascending from legs to arms to face (over days to 4 weeks), areflexia, mild sensory symptoms, autonomic dysfunction (arrhythmias, BP instability). Peak weakness at 2-4 weeks. Respiratory muscle involvement in 25% requiring mechanical ventilation. CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal WBC). Mortality 3-7%, mostly from complications.
